en.extract:
Gilbert's whistler (Pachycephala inornata) is a monotypic species of bird endemic to Australia, scattered in semi-arid zones of southern Australia.


Taxonomy
The family Pachycephalidae originated within the Australo-Papuan region. Together with the red-lored whistler and the olive whistler, Gilbert's whistler is basal to the genus Pachycephala – the typical whistlers. This old monotypic lineage represents a relictual form that was once more widespread.
John Gould, who first described this species in 1841, named it after Mr Gilbert who discovered the bird in Western Australia and collected specimens that he handed over to Gould. As for its scientific name – Pachycephala inornata – in Ancient Greek pachys and kephale respectively mean 'thick' and 'head'; several of the members of the genus Pachycephala are indeed called 'thickheads', and other common names for Gilbert's whistler include black-lored Gilbert thickhead, red-throated thickhead, Gilbert's thickhead, or simply thickhead. With 'inornatus' meaning 'plain' in Latin, the second part of its binominal name is attributed to Gilbert's whistler's plumage, which is often described in the literature as being plain.
There are several entries in the GenBank DNA sequence database for Pachycephala inornata.


Description
The adult male, which weighs 29.8-32.5 g, possesses a rufous-coloured chin and throat from its third year of life, as does its most likely closest relative, the red-lored whistler. Both males can easily be differentiated by the colour of their lores: black for the Gilbert's and red for the red-lored whistler. The female Gilbert's whistler (23.5-32.2 g) has a pale grey throat and a white ring around the eye; her underparts can be lightly streaked. Both sexes are uniformly brownish-grey, and have a red iris and a black stubby bill (17–18 mm). Juveniles (30.9 g) are darker than adults, with tinges of brown, and have dark streaks on the breast and belly. Immatures are very similar to adult females except for their brown bill and dark brown iris. Care should be taken not to mistake the female and immature Gilbert's whistler for the female golden or western whistlers where they co-exist.
At one stage, Gilbert's whistler was divided into two subspecies: Pachycephala inornata inornata to the east of the Flinders Ranges with greyish white underparts, and Pachycephala inornata gilberti in the west with cinnamon and buff underparts; this variation is now considered clinal.
A study undertaken by Onley, Gardner and Symonds (2020) on possible larger appendages in whistlers caused by climate change (Allen's rule) from museum specimens collected between 1915 and 2013, found an increase in the body size of the Gilbert's whistler at higher latitudes, following Bergmann's rule.


Ecology


Diet
Gilbert's whistlers feed mostly on the ground, as does about half of all declining species of birds in southern Australia. This species also forages in understorey layers. They feed primarily on invertebrates, although fruit and seeds can also form part of its diet.


Nesting and clutch
Gilbert's whistlers erect a deep cup nest in a dense shrub, often on top of old babblers' nests. Both sexes build the nest, incubate two or three, sometimes four eggs over a period of 15 days, and look after the chicks. Young are altricial and nidicolous.


Vocalisations
All whistlers are songsters, and Gilbert's whistler in particular, is more often heard than seen. Its loud, melodious and clear call can be heard at distances up to 900 m, mostly during the breeding season when it establishes a breeding territory. Its song is described as a series of swelling cheop, cheop, cheop repeated five to 20 times, with another ascending call er-WHIT, er-WHIT, er-WHIT similar to the rufous whistler, and also a more scratchy call eechowk, eechOWK, eeCHOWK.


Habitat, distribution and threats
Gilbert's whistler inhabits semi-arid tall mallee with sparse shrubby understorey or prickly Acacia thickets and Casuarina woodlands, and is also found in thickets of Melaleuca and, occasionally, in taller eucalypt woodlands or forests. A study undertaken in 2017 on the effect of bushfires and prescribed burns on the distribution of 12 threatened bird species predicted that habitat burnt between 30 and 60 years approximately would be favoured by Gilbert's whistlers while habitat burnt more frequently or not burnt for more than 60 years would not readily suit this species.
The distribution of Gilbert's whistlers is scattered in the semi-arid parts of southern Western Australia and South Australia, northwest Victoria and central west New South Wales. No large scale movements were recorded for the species which is thought to be resident or sedentary with a high degree of site fidelity. It is therefore not surprising that this species is sensitive to disturbance, as described by Murphy following a study on the conservation value of small woodland remnants in New South Wales, where he noted that the distribution of Gilbert's whistler in this region is largely restricted to the remaining original vegetation. Another project undertaken by a bird banding group in the Charcoal Tank Nature Reserve in central west New South Wales sadly recorded the extinction of Gilbert's whistler in this 86-hectare (210-acre) patch of remnant vegetation during the 'Big Dry' in 2000–01. This local extinction, a classic example of the 'Extinction Debt', was mainly attributed to: habitat fragmentation, land clearing for agriculture during the last 100 years, introduced predators, increased competition from other native species, and drought periods exacerbated by climate change. Additional details on these threats are outlined on the NSW Government species profile page.


Conservation
Of the four states in which Gilbert's whistler occurs, this species is listed as vulnerable only in New South Wales under the NSW Biodiversity Conservation Act 2016 based on population decrease in size and distribution. A conservation strategy was developed in 2015 as part of the Saving our Species program with some of the priority management areas including: restoring native vegetation, removing weeds, implementing actions to reduce the impact of noisy miners when deemed necessary, encouraging landholders to implement sensitive grazing practices, strip burning with refuge areas remaining unburnt for 25 years or more, raising public awareness of the importance of large old trees, revegetating, and identifying methods to improve soil quality.
Gilbert's whistler is not listed at national level under the Environment Protection and Biodiversity Conservation Act 1999, and is listed as least concern at international level under the IUCN Red List of Threatened Species 2016.


Gallery


References


External links
Recordings and images on eBird
Sonograms from Xeno-canto
Images and recording by Graeme Chapman

'''Gilbert's whistler''' ('''''Pachycephala inornata''''') is a [[Monotypic taxon|monotypic]] [[species]] of [[bird]] [[Endemism|endemic]] to [[Australia]], scattered in [[Semi-arid climate|semi-arid]] zones of southern Australia.<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|last=Higgins, P. J. and Peter J. M. (Eds.)|title=Handbook of Australian, New Zealand and Antarctic Birds. Volume 6: Pardalotes to Shrikethrushes|publisher=Oxford University Press|year=2002|location=Melbourne|pages=1096–1100}}</ref><ref name=":1">{{Cite journal|last1=Jønsson|first1=Knud Andreas|last2=Irestedt|first2=Martin|last3=Christidis|first3=Les|last4=Clegg|first4=Sonya M.|last5=Holt|first5=Ben G.|last6=Fjeldså|first6=Jon|date=2014-02-22|title=Evidence of taxon cycles in an Indo-Pacific passerine bird radiation (Aves: Pachycephala)|journal=Proceedings of the Royal Society B: Biological Sciences|volume=281|issue=1777|article-number=20131727|doi=10.1098/rspb.2013.1727|pmc=3896003|pmid=24403319}}</ref>

== Taxonomy ==
The family [[Pachycephalidae]] originated within the Australo-Papuan region.<ref name=":1" /><ref name=":2">{{Cite journal|last1=Jønsson|first1=Knud A.|last2=Bowie|first2=Rauri C. K.|last3=Moyle|first3=Robert G.|last4=Christidis|first4=Les|last5=Norman|first5=Janette A.|last6=Benz|first6=Brett W.|last7=Fjeldså|first7=Jon|date=2010|title=Historical biogeography of an Indo-Pacific passerine bird family (Pachycephalidae): different colonization patterns in the Indonesian and Melanesian archipelagos|url=http://doi.wiley.com/10.1111/j.1365-2699.2009.02220.x|journal=Journal of Biogeography|language=en|volume=37|issue=2|pages=245–257|doi=10.1111/j.1365-2699.2009.02220.x|bibcode=2010JBiog..37..245J |s2cid=85013290 |url-access=subscription}}</ref> Together with the [[red-lored whistler]] and the [[olive whistler]], Gilbert's whistler is [[Basal (phylogenetics)|basal]] to the [[genus]] [[Pachycephala]] – the typical whistlers.<ref name=":1" /><ref name=":2" /> This old [[Monotypic taxon|monotypic]] [[Lineage (anthropology)|lineage]] represents a [[Relict (biology)|relictual]] form that was once more widespread.<ref name=":1" />

[[John Gould]], who first described this [[species]] in 1841, named it after Mr Gilbert who discovered the [[bird]] in [[Western Australia]] and collected [[Zoological specimen|specimens]] that he handed over to Gould.<ref>{{Cite book|last1=Gould|first1=Elizabeth|url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/item/187062|title=The birds of Australia.|last2=Gould|first2=John|last3=Richter|first3=Henry Constantine|date=1840|publisher=Printed by R. and J. E. Taylor; pub. by the author|volume=2|location=London}}</ref> As for its [[Binomial nomenclature|scientific name]] – ''Pachycephala inornata'' – in [[Ancient Greek]] ''pachys'' and ''kephale'' respectively mean 'thick' and 'head'; several of the members of the [[genus]] [[Pachycephala]] are indeed called 'thickheads', and other [[common name]]s for Gilbert's whistler include black-lored Gilbert thickhead, red-throated thickhead, Gilbert's thickhead, or simply thickhead.<ref name=":0" /> With 'inornatus' meaning 'plain' in [[Latin]], the second part of its [[Binomial nomenclature|binominal name]] is attributed to Gilbert's whistler's [[plumage]], which is often described in the literature as being plain.

There are several entries in the [[GenBank]] DNA sequence database for ''Pachycephala inornata.''<ref>{{Cite web|title=pachycephala inornata - Nucleotide - NCBI|url=https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/nuccore/?term=pachycephala+inornata|access-date=2021-06-03|website=www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov|language=en}}</ref>
[[File:Gould & Richter lithograph Pachycephala inornata.jpg|alt=Gilbert's Pachycephala in The birds of Australia (1840) by John Gould|thumb|Gould and Richter lithograph of male (top) and female (below) Gilbert's whistler]]

== Description ==
The adult male, which weighs 29.8-32.5 g,<ref name=":0" /> possesses a rufous-coloured chin and throat<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":5">{{Cite web|title=Gilbert's Whistler {{!}} BirdLife Australia|url=https://www.birdlife.org.au/bird-profile/gilberts-whistler|access-date=2021-06-03|website=www.birdlife.org.au|language=en|archive-date=2021-06-03|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210603212546/https://www.birdlife.org.au/bird-profile/gilberts-whistler}}</ref><ref name=":4">{{Cite web|title=Gilbert's Whistler - profile {{!}} NSW Environment, Energy and Science|url=https://www.environment.nsw.gov.au/threatenedSpeciesApp/profile.aspx?id=10582|access-date=2021-06-03|website=www.environment.nsw.gov.au}}</ref><ref name=":3">{{Cite book|last=Menkhorst, P., Rogers, D., Clarke, R., Davies, J., Marsack, P., Franklin, K.|title=The Australian bird guide, Whistlers|publisher=CSIRO Publishing|year=2017|location=Clayton South, VIC|pages=476–481}}</ref><ref name=":6">{{Cite book|last=Pizzey, G and Knight, F.|title=Birds of Australia, Digital edition|publisher=Gibbon Multimedia|year=2013}}</ref> from its third year of life,<ref name=":0" /> as does its most likely closest relative,<ref name=":2" /> the [[red-lored whistler]]. Both males can easily be differentiated by the colour of their [[Lore (anatomy)|lores]]: black for the Gilbert's and red for the [[red-lored whistler]]. The female Gilbert's whistler (23.5-32.2 g) has a pale grey throat and a white ring around the eye; her underparts can be lightly streaked.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":3" /> Both sexes are uniformly brownish-grey, and have a red [[Iris (anatomy)|iris]] and a black stubby [[Beak|bill]] (17–18 mm).<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":3" /> [[Juvenile (organism)|Juveniles]] (30.9 g) are darker than adults, with tinges of brown, and have dark streaks on the breast and belly.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":3" /> Immatures are very similar to adult females except for their brown [[Beak|bill]] and dark brown [[Iris (anatomy)|iris]].<ref name=":0" /> Care should be taken not to mistake the female and immature Gilbert's whistler for the female [[Australian golden whistler|golden]] or [[western whistler]]s where they co-exist.<ref name=":0" />

At one stage, Gilbert's whistler was divided into two [[subspecies]]: ''Pachycephala inornata inornata'' to the east of the [[Flinders Ranges]] with greyish white underparts, and ''Pachycephala inornata gilberti'' in the west with cinnamon and buff underparts;<ref>{{Cite journal|last=Ford|first=Julian|date=1971-07-01|title=Subspeciation in the Gilbert Whistler|journal=Emu - Austral Ornithology|volume=71|issue=3|pages=141–142|doi=10.1071/MU971140f|bibcode=1971EmuAO..71..141F |issn=0158-4197}}</ref> this variation is now considered [[Cline (biology)|clinal]].<ref name=":0" />

A study undertaken by Onley, Gardner and Symonds (2020) on possible larger [[appendage]]s in whistlers caused by [[climate change]] ([[Allen's rule]]) from [[Zoological specimen|museum specimen]]s collected between 1915 and 2013, found an increase in the body size of the Gilbert's whistler at higher [[latitude]]s, following [[Bergmann's rule]].<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Onley|first1=Isabelle R|last2=Gardner|first2=Janet L|last3=Symonds|first3=Matthew R E|date=2020-05-05|title=Spatial and temporal variation in morphology in Australian whistlers and shrike-thrushes: is climate change causing larger appendages?|journal=Biological Journal of the Linnean Society|volume=130|issue=1|pages=101–113|doi=10.1093/biolinnean/blaa028|issn=0024-4066|hdl=1885/216791|hdl-access=free}}</ref>
[[File:Distribution Map Gilbert's whistler (Pachycephala inornata).jpg|alt=Distribution Map Gilbert's whistler (Pachycephala inornata)|thumb|Distribution Map Gilbert's whistler (Pachycephala inornata)]]
[[File:Male Gilbert's Whistler (Pachycephala inornata).jpg|alt=Photo taken at AWC Buckaringa Wildlife Sanctuary, South Australia, by Cameron Whiley in November 2025.|thumb|Photo taken at AWC Buckaringa Wildlife Sanctuary, South Australia, by Cameron Whiley in November 2025.]]

== Ecology ==

=== Diet ===
Gilbert's whistlers feed mostly on the ground,<ref name=":0" /> as does about half of all declining species of birds in southern Australia.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Antos|first1=Mark J.|last2=Bennett|first2=Andrew F.|date=2005|title=How important are different types of temperate woodlands for ground-foraging birds?|url=http://www.publish.csiro.au/?paper=WR04118|journal=Wildlife Research|language=en|volume=32|issue=6|page=557|doi=10.1071/WR04118|bibcode=2005WildR..32..557A |issn=1035-3712|url-access=subscription}}</ref> This species also forages in [[Understory|understorey]] layers.<ref name=":0" /> They feed primarily on [[invertebrate]]s, although [[fruit]] and [[seed]]s can also form part of its diet.<ref name=":0" />

=== Nesting and clutch ===
Gilbert's whistlers erect a deep [[Bird nest|cup nest]] in a dense shrub,<ref name=":0" /> often on top of old [[Australo-Papuan babbler|babblers]]' [[Bird nest|nests]].<ref name=":0" /><ref>{{Cite journal|last=Chandler|first=L. G.|date=1941-01-01|title=A Note on the Gilbert Whistler|journal=Emu - Austral Ornithology|volume=40|issue=4|page=323|doi=10.1071/MU940323a|bibcode=1941EmuAO..40..323C |issn=0158-4197}}</ref><ref name=":7">{{Cite journal|last=Hunt, Anthony; Ewin, Peter; Clayton, Mark|date=2018|title=An example of a "Twinkling Lights" local extinction event: population dynamics of Gilbert's Whistler at The Charcoal Tank Nature Reserve, New South Wales|journal=Corella|volume=42|pages=42–49}}</ref> Both sexes build the [[Bird nest|nest]], [[Egg incubation|incubate]] two or three, sometimes four [[Bird egg|eggs]] over a period of 15 days, and look after the [[Bird|chicks]].<ref name=":0" /> Young are [[altricial]] and [[nidicolous]].<ref name=":0" />

== Vocalisations ==
All whistlers are [[Songbird|songsters]], and Gilbert's whistler in particular, is more often heard than seen.<ref name=":3" /> Its loud, melodious and clear [[Bird vocalization|call]] can be heard at distances up to 900 m,<ref name=":0" /> mostly during the breeding season<ref name=":3" /> when it establishes a breeding territory.<ref name=":0" /> Its [[Bird vocalization|song]] is described as a series of swelling ''cheop, cheop, cheop'' repeated five to 20 times,<ref name=":3" /> with another ascending call ''er-WHIT, er-WHIT, er-WHIT''<ref>{{Cite journal|last=O'Neill|first=W. J.|date=1936-04-01|title=Gilbert Whistler|journal=Emu - Austral Ornithology|volume=35|issue=4|page=358|doi=10.1071/MU935356d|bibcode=1936EmuAO..35..358O |issn=0158-4197}}</ref> similar to the [[rufous whistler]],<ref name=":3" /> and also a more scratchy call ''eechowk, eechOWK, eeCHOWK''.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Michael.|first=Morcombe|title=Michael Morcombe's birds of Australia.|date=1991|publisher=Book Company in association with Ure Smith|isbn=1-86309-002-9|oclc=27621009}}</ref>

== Habitat, distribution and threats ==
Gilbert's whistler inhabits [[Semi-arid climate|semi-arid]] tall [[Mallee (habit)|mallee]] with sparse shrubby [[Understory|understorey]] or prickly ''[[Acacia]]'' thickets and ''[[Casuarina]]'' woodlands, and is also found in thickets of ''[[Melaleuca]]'' and, occasionally, in taller eucalypt woodlands or forests.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":2" /><ref name=":4" /> A study undertaken in 2017 on the effect of [[Wildfire|bushfires]] and [[Controlled burn|prescribed burns]] on the distribution of 12 [[Threatened fauna of Australia|threatened]] [[Bird|bird species]] predicted that [[habitat]] burnt between 30 and 60 years approximately would be favoured by Gilbert's whistlers while [[habitat]] burnt more frequently or not burnt for more than 60 years would not readily suit this species.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Connell|first1=Jemima|last2=Watson|first2=Simon J.|last3=Taylor|first3=Rick S.|last4=Avitabile|first4=Sarah C.|last5=Clarke|first5=Rohan H.|last6=Bennett|first6=Andrew F.|last7=Clarke|first7=Michael F.|date=2017|editor-last=Elith|editor-first=Jane|title=Testing the effects of a century of fires: Requirements for post-fire succession predict the distribution of threatened bird species|journal=Diversity and Distributions|language=en|volume=23|issue=9|pages=1078–1089|doi=10.1111/ddi.12597|doi-access=free|bibcode=2017DivDi..23.1078C }}</ref>

The [[Species distribution|distribution]] of Gilbert's whistlers is scattered in the [[Semi-arid climate|semi-arid]] parts of southern [[Western Australia]] and [[South Australia]], northwest [[Victoria (Australia)|Victoria]] and central west [[New South Wales]].<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":5" /><ref name=":3" /><ref name=":6" /> No large scale movements were recorded for the species which is thought to be [[Bird migration|resident]] or sedentary<ref name=":0" /> with a high degree of [[Philopatry|site fidelity]].<ref>{{Cite book|last=Reid|first=J. R. W.|title=Threatened and declining birds in the New South Wales Sheep-Wheat Belt: II. Landscape relationships – modelling bird atlas data against vegetation cover. Consultancy report to NSW National Parks and Wildlife Service.|publisher=CSIRO Sustainable Ecosystems|year=2000|location=Canberra}}</ref> It is therefore not surprising that this species is sensitive to [[Disturbance (ecology)|disturbance]], as described by Murphy following a study on the conservation value of small woodland remnants in [[New South Wales]], where he noted that the [[Species distribution|distribution]] of Gilbert's whistler in this region is largely restricted to the remaining original [[vegetation]].<ref>{{Cite journal|last=Murphy|first=Michael|date=2014|title=The conservation value of small woodland remnants on the New South Wales South Western Slopes: a case study from Wagga Wagga|journal=Australian Zoologist|volume=31|issue=1|pages=71–81|doi=10.7882/AZ.1999.008|issn=0067-2238}}</ref> Another project undertaken by a [[Bird ringing|bird banding]] group in the [[Charcoal Tank Nature Reserve]] in central west [[New South Wales]] sadly recorded the [[extinction]] of Gilbert's whistler in this {{convert|86|hectare|acre|adj=on}} patch of remnant [[vegetation]] during the '[[Drought in Australia|Big Dry]]' in 2000–01.<ref name=":7" /> This [[local extinction]], a classic example of the '[[Extinction debt|Extinction Debt]]', was mainly attributed to: [[habitat fragmentation]], [[Land clearing in Australia|land clearing]] for agriculture during the last 100 years, [[Introduced species|introduced predators]], increased [[Competition (biology)|competition]] from other native species, and [[drought]] periods exacerbated by [[climate change]]. Additional details on these [[Threats to biodiversity|threats]] are outlined on the [[Government of New South Wales|NSW Government]] species profile page.<ref name=":4" />

== Conservation ==
Of the four states in which Gilbert's whistler occurs, this species is listed as vulnerable only in [[New South Wales]] under the [[Biodiversity Conservation Act 2016 (NSW)|NSW Biodiversity Conservation Act 2016]] based on population decrease in size and [[Species distribution|distribution]].<ref name=":4" /> A [[Conservation biology|conservation]] strategy was developed in 2015 as part of the Saving our Species program with some of the priority management areas including: [[Restoration ecology|restoring]] [[Native species|native vegetation]], removing [[weed]]s, implementing actions to reduce the impact of [[noisy miner]]s when deemed necessary, encouraging landholders to implement sensitive [[Grazing (behaviour)|grazing]] practices, strip [[Controlled burn|burning]] with [[Refuge (ecology)|refuge]] areas remaining unburnt for 25 years or more, raising [[Public awareness of science|public awareness]] of the importance of large old trees, [[Revegetation|revegetating]], and identifying methods to improve [[soil quality]].<ref>{{Cite web|title=Gilbert's Whistler (Pachycephala inornata) {{!}} Conservation project {{!}} NSW Environment, Energy and Science|url=https://www.environment.nsw.gov.au/savingourspeciesapp/Project.aspx?results=c&ProfileID=10582|access-date=2021-06-04|website=www.environment.nsw.gov.au}}</ref>

Gilbert's whistler is not listed at national level under the [[Environment Protection and Biodiversity Conservation Act 1999]],<ref>{{Cite web|last=Environment|first=jurisdiction=Commonwealth of Australia; corporateName=Department of the|title=Threatened Species List|url=http://www.environment.gov.au/cgi-bin/sprat/public/publicthreatenedlist.pl#birds_endangered|access-date=2021-06-04|website=www.environment.gov.au|language=en}}</ref> and is listed as [[Least-concern species|least concern]] at international level under the [[IUCN Red List|IUCN Red List of Threatened Species 2016]].<ref>{{Cite web|author=BirdLife International|date=2016-10-01|title=IUCN Red List of Threatened Species: Pachycephala inornata|url=https://www.iucnredlist.org/en|access-date=2021-06-04|website=IUCN Red List of Threatened Species}}</ref>

zh.extract:
吉氏啸鹟（学名：Pachycephala inornata），是啸鹟科啸鹟属的一种，为澳大利亚的特有种。该物种的保护状况被评为无危。
吉氏啸鹟的平均体重约为30.8克。栖息地包括亚热带或热带的旱林、干燥的稀树草原、亚热带或热带的（低地）干燥疏灌丛和地中海型疏灌丛。


参考文献
